coredata.dc:title: Tube-on-rod structure of MWCNTs-ammonium cobalt vanadium oxide/ammonium vanadium oxide; evaluation of electrochemical hydrogen storage capacity
coredata.dc:creator: Gomrokchi, Poria
Shabani-Nooshabadi, Mehdi
Ghiyasiyan-Arani, Maryam
Salavati-Niasari, Masoud
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanostructures have lately been widely used in energy storage systems. This study examines the impact of ammonium cobalt vanadium oxide/ammonium vanadium oxide as electrode materials in hydrogen storage which modifying with carbon nanostructures. The poor conductivity of vanadate-based materials may be improved by adding zero to two-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), graphene oxide (GO), and multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs). Electrode materials based on (NH4)2V12O27·xH2O/(NH4)2Co2V10O28·16H2O/NH4V3O8 were synthesized by hydrothermal method in the presence of PEG with engineered morphology of one dimensional (ID) nanorods. The carbonous nanocomposites based on GQDs, MWCNTs and GO exhibit a capacity 203, 438 and 28 mAhg−1 after 15 cycles at a current density of 1 mA. The hydrogen storage performance is influenced by various amount of MWCNTs. Increasing the percentage of MWCNTs led to decreasing the capacity. Therefore, optimum electrode materials fabricated in the presence of 5 % MWCNTs which has 1055 mAhg−1 capacity. Therefore, 1D/1D structure of Tube-on-Rod can improve the performance of hydrogen storage in the ammonium cobalt vanadium oxide/ammonium vanadium oxide electrodes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical hydrogen storage
One-dimension
Conductivity
Hydrothermal synthesis
Tube-on-rod
Vanadate-based nanostructures

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Professor Masoud Salavati-Niasari and co-authors of the Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, IRAN are friendly approved for providing access to the analysis facility by grant No 159271/PG1 from University of Kashan and the council of Iran National Science Foundation (INSF, 97017837 ). Authors would like to thank Miss Angham Husseini (SAMA Research Center) for her valuable help in making and supporting SAMA 500 Electroanalyzer instrument.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD diffractogram for prepared nanocomposites in the presence of (a) GO (S1), (b) GQDs (S2) and (c) MWCNTs (S3).
Williamson-Hall plot extracted from XRD data for prepared nanocomposites in the presence of (a) GO (S1), (b) GQDs (S2) and (c) MWCNTs (S3).
FT-IR spectra for (a) pristine sample and prepared nanocomposites in the presence of (b) GO (S1), (c) GQDs (S2) and (d) MWCNTs (S3).
FE-SEM micrographs for prepared in the presence of (a, b) GO (S1), (c, d) GQDs (S2) and (e, f) MWCNTs (S3).
EDS results for prepared nanocomposites in the presence of (a) GO (S1), (b) GQDs (S2) and (c) MWCNTs (S3).
HR-TEM images for synthesized nanocomposite in the presence of MWCNTs.
BET and BJH plot for synthesized nanocomposites in the presence of (a, b) GO (S1), (c, d) GQDs (S2) and (e, f) MWCNTs (S3).
Cyclic voltammograms for designed nanocomposites (a) S1, (b) S2 and (c) S3, (d) S4 and (e) S5.
Discharge profiles for nanocomposites based on (a) 10 % GO (S1), (b) 10 % GQDs (S2), (c) 10 % MWCNTs (S3), (d) 20 % MWCNTs (S4) and (e) 5 % MWCNTs (S5).
Column plot for discharge capacity of nanocomposites based on (a) 10 % GO (S1), (b) 10 % GQDs (S2), (c) 10 % MWCNTs (S3), (d) 20 % MWCNTs (S4) and (e) 5 % MWCNTs (S5).
Schematic illustration for formation mechanism of vanadate materials and representative carbonous nanocomposites.
Derived data from BET analysis of designed nanocomposites.
Electrode response for CV and ChP test of obtained samples.
Comparison of different electrode materials applied in hydrogen storage systems with the results of this work.

head.ce:title: Tube-on-rod structure of MWCNTs-ammonium cobalt vanadium oxide/ammonium vanadium oxide; evaluation of electrochemical hydrogen storage capacity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, Kashan 87317-51167, Islamic Republic of Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanostructures have lately been widely used in energy storage systems. This study examines the impact of ammonium cobalt vanadium oxide/ammonium vanadium oxide as electrode materials in hydrogen storage which modifying with carbon nanostructures. The poor conductivity of vanadate-based materials may be improved by adding zero to two-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), graphene oxide (GO), and multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs). Electrode materials based on (NH4)2V12O27·xH2O/(NH4)2Co2V10O28·16H2O/NH4V3O8 were synthesized by hydrothermal method in the presence of PEG with engineered morphology of one dimensional (ID) nanorods. The carbonous nanocomposites based on GQDs, MWCNTs and GO exhibit a capacity 203, 438 and 28 mAhg−1 after 15 cycles at a current density of 1 mA. The hydrogen storage performance is influenced by various amount of MWCNTs. Increasing the percentage of MWCNTs led to decreasing the capacity. Therefore, optimum electrode materials fabricated in the presence of 5 % MWCNTs which has 1055 mAhg−1 capacity. Therefore, 1D/1D structure of Tube-on-Rod can improve the performance of hydrogen storage in the ammonium cobalt vanadium oxide/ammonium vanadium oxide electrodes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy is generated via two distinct methods: renewable and non-renewable. Approximately 80 % of today's energy is generated through renewable means, which possess considerable power and resilience. However, the world is confronted with significant challenges such as escalating environmental pollution, soaring fuel costs, accelerated global warming, and the imminent depletion of non-renewable resources [1–3]. Several research have been conducted to address the aforementioned issues related to energy storage. It has become imperative and crucial to expand the use of clean and sustainable energy sources for the purpose of storing energy [4].

Hydrogen is a clean and renewable fuel that is abundant and non-toxic. When burned, it produces water vapor, which is harmless to the environment and humans. This process eliminates the need for non-renewable oil resources and reduces greenhouse gas emissions, ultimately leading to a decrease in pollution levels. Hydrogen has long been used as a fuel source for automobiles, balloons, and rockets. Its peak usage occurred in the 1980s, coinciding with the onset of the oil crisis [5]. The superior features of hydrogen, which make it more important than non-renewable sources such as other hydrocarbons, can be attributed to its high chemical energy, weight that is three times greater than gasoline, volume that is four times less than gasoline, and energy density that is three times lower than natural gas. In summary, we have determined that hydrogen can serve as a viable alternative to fossil fuels [6,7].

The economic infrastructure of hydrogen refers to the necessary systems and facilities that enable the use of hydrogen as an alternative to fossil fuels. This includes the production, distribution, storage, conversion, and use of hydrogen. Hydrogen generation may be derived from several sources, such as Biological sources [8], wind [9], Thermolysis of water [10], Electrolysis of water [11], Photo-splitting of water [12], Reforming fossil fuels, and Methanol [13].

The hydrogen storage mechanism involves the utilization of various materials under different conditions of temperature and pressure. This can be achieved through chemical storage, where hydrogen is retained in substances like formic acid and ammonia. Alternatively, physisorption storage can be employed using carbon-based materials such as fullerene, nanotubes, graphene, and zeolites like metal-organic frameworks, covalent organic frameworks (COFs), micro-porous metal coordination materials (MMOMS), clathrate, and organotransition metal complexes [7].

The choice of electrode material is crucial in determining the performance of energy storage devices. The electrode material is manufactured using several processes such as electro-polymerization/electrodeposition [14], in-situ polymerization [15], direct coating [16], sol-gel [17], chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [18], vacuum filtering [19], hydrothermal/solvothermal [20], and dealloying [21]. The electrode materials must possess high conductivity, excellent temperature and chemical stability (inertness), corrosion resistance, environmental friendliness, and affordability. Common electrode materials include carbon compounds, metal oxides, and conductive polymers. The potential materials might be carbon-based substances, namely carbon nanomaterials, that possess desirable properties such as strong electrical conductivity, chemical and electrochemical stability, and cost-effectiveness in terms of manufacturing. Furthermore, the favorable rectangular form of CV curves and the symmetrical galvanostatic charge/discharge diagram of carbon materials indicate that carbon-based materials are appropriate for use as capacitive materials. The primary energy storage mechanism of carbon material is the formation of a double layer, which does not involve any chemical reactions. Carbon material appears as a single color (black) and does not possess electrochromic properties [22].

The present study focuses on the synthesis of (NH4)2Co2V10O28·16H2O structures using a simple hydrothermal method, known for its ability to produce particles with exceptional purity, crystallinity, and controlled chemical and physical properties [23]. Additionally, the study investigates the application of these structures, along with graphene oxide (GO), graphene quantum dots (GQD), and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT), as electrode materials for hydrogen storage. The Ammonium Cobalt Vanadium Oxide materials and its composites exhibit impressive capabilities.

Vanadium-based compounds exhibit significant superiority compared to manganese-based oxides [24], poly-anionic compounds [25], and other vanadium-based compounds [26,27]. This is attributed to their layered structure and high theoretical capacity capabilities. Vanadium oxide is a highly used transition-metal oxide in many industries due to its diverse compositions (V2O5, V3O7, V4O9, V6O13, VO2, V2O3, etc.) with varying oxidation states (+5, +4, +3, +2). Energy storage researchers find them appealing because to their abundant availability, inexpensive cost, and wide variety of possible applications. On top of that, the distinct composition of various vanadium oxides allows for the replacement or addition of other metal ions (such as Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Li+, Na+), while still maintaining electrical balance throughout the process of ion intercalation and deintercalation [28–30]. The reduction of the high-valence vanadium ion to a low-valence vanadium ion ensures the electrical neutrality of the oxides is maintained during ion intercalation. Furthermore, the rapid deterioration caused by the inadequate electrical conductivity of these materials poses a challenge for energy storage use. One option is to use 1D heterostructures as a means to improve both electrical conductivity and structural durability [31,32].

Weina Deng et al. produced the (NH4)2Co2V10O28·16H2O/(NH4)2V10O25·8H2O compound using a hydrothermal technique. This compound was used as a cathode in high-performance aqueous Zn-ion batteries. This electrode demonstrates a higher capacity of 390.1 mAh g−1 at a rate of 0.1 A g−1 and maintains a discharge capacity of 201.0 mAh g−1 even after undergoing 1000 cycles, with a capacity retention rate of 82.1 % [33]. Ayat Allah Mamdouh et al. synthesized (NH4)2Co2V10O28·16H2O using the precipitation technique and conducted research to evaluate its antibacterial properties [34]. Vanadium-based materials has several advantages, including environmental benignity, high safety, abundance in the earth's crust (about 300 times more than lithium), multiple redox processes, low cost, and a layered structure with customizable tunnels. The presence of hydrogen bonds (N–H/O) between the NH4+ and VO layers improves the structural stability of the layered structure composed of VO layers, interstitial NH4+, and H2O. This enhancement in cohesion helps to mitigate structural changes during the charge/discharge cycling process. In reality, it plays a crucial role in ensuring the long-term stability [35,36].

Furthermore, because vanadate materials have poor rate proficiency and electrochemical stability, engineering nanomaterials as a practical means of enhancing electrochemical performance is an essential tactic to ensure excellent electroactive sites and a quick electron/ion diffusion route. Carbon-based nanocomposites, such as carbon nanotube networks [37], graphene sheet [38], and carbon nitrides [39], are employed as conductive substrates to address this issue. Specifically, transition metal oxide-based materials can be coupled with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) that have a high surface area and sp2-bonded carbon atoms, via their functional groups. This gives the hybrid nanostructures unique structural features and complementary electrochemical properties that come from both counterparts. Recently, functionalized GQDs with noteworthy physicochemical characteristics have surfaced for various operational applications, particularly in energy storage [40,41]. The use of GQDs in the energy storage platform has not received much attention. Nonetheless, the use of these carbon-based substances in electrochemical energy storage systems, such as supercapacitors, Li-ion, Na-ion, and Li-S batteries, has been shown [42–44]. Conversely, carbon microspheres have advantageous properties for energy-related applications [45]. 1D carbon materials, namely graphene-based fibers, carbon fibers and CNTs have garnered significant attention of scientific community to build the flexible and wearable supercapacitor devices [46–48].

We used ammonium cobalt vanadium oxide/ammonium vanadium oxide as an electrode material to its unique characteristics. The ammonium cobalt vanadium oxide/ammonium vanadium oxide were designed with ID structures in the presence of carbonous compounds for overcome low conductivity of vanadate-based materials. The composites of different carbon nanostructures with diverse dimensions (0D, ID and 2D) were designed with nanorods vanadate structures as potential hydrogen storage electrode materials. The ammonium cobalt vanadium oxide/ammonium vanadium oxide exhibited a 1D nanorod morphology, whereas the MWCNTs had a 1D structure. These two materials produced an interconnected 1D/1D heterostructure of Tube-on-Rod. The MWCNTs based nanocomposites as optimal electrode exhibited a capacity of 1055 mAh g−1 at 1 mA after 15 cycles.

The chemical precursors and starting materials utilized in the synthesis process of samples including, cobalt acetate tetrahydrate (99.0–101.0 %), ammonium vanadate (≥99.0 %), oxalic acid (98 %), and PEG 400 were purchased from a Merck company and applied as arrived without further purification. The carbonous materials of MWCNTs and GO were purchased from Merck company and GQDs were provided based on previous literature [49].

In order to the synthesis of ammonium cobalt vanadium oxide, hydrothermal procedure applied in the presence of PEG. First, the 0.5 g (0.005 mmol) ammonium vanadate dissolved in 30 ml DI water at 80 °C, and the oxalic acid (0.43 g-0.004 mmol) solution added to vanadate solution which led to prepare orange solution (Mixture A). Then 0.208 g (0.001 mmol) cobalt acetate tetrahydrate dissolved in o.2 g PEG (Mixture B). Finally, the mixture B added to mixture A and stirred for 1 h. The obtained dark orange solution transferred to an autoclave and heated for 3 h at 180 °C. The resultant precipitate centrifuged, washed with DI water and methanol several times and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. Carbonous nanocomposites were prepared by dispersing the resultant products in methanol in the presence of different carbon-based compounds as GO, GQDs and MWCNTs (with amount of 10 %) which denote as S1, S2 and S3. The provided nanocomposites with 20 % and 5 % of MWCNTs denoted as S4 and S5, respectively. Then, the mixture was stirred at ambient temperature for 8 h. The resultant nanocomposites dried in an oven at 60 °C.

The XRD patterns of products were recorded by a Philips-X'pertpro, X-ray diffractometer using Ni-filtered Cu Ka radiation. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on Nicolet Magna- 550 spectrometer in KBr pellets. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained on LEO-1455VP equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy. The EDX analysis with 20 kV accelerated voltage was administered. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was obtained on a Philips EM208 transmission electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The N2 adsorption/desorption analysis (BET) was performed in −196 °C using an automated gas adsorption analyzer (Tristar 3000, Micromeritics). Pore size distribution was calculated by using desorption branch of the isotherm by the Barrett, Joyner and Halenda (BJH) method.

The hydrogen storage capacity and electrochemical properties of each sample were assessed using the chronopotentiometry technique in a three-electrode electrochemical cell. The cell consisted of a working electrode, a Pt counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl reference electrode. The electrolyte used was a 2.0 M KOH solution. The working electrode was fabricated by applying a thin layer of the as-synthesized powder samples onto a substrate composed of pure copper plate. The coating solution was generated by dispersing as-synthesized nanostructures in ethanol, which was sonicated 15 min in an ultrasonic bath to create a homogeneous dispersion. To obtain a thin layer, this solution was applied onto the surface of a copper substrate using a pipette and then dried at a temperature of 80 °C, without the use of any binder. An electrochemical cell was established under ambient conditions. The potential of the working electrode was measured in relation to the reference electrode by delivering a continuous current between the working and counter electrodes.

Crystalline structure of resultant samples was characterized using X-Ray diffraction pattern which demonstrate in Fig. 1 (a–c) for synthesized nanocomposites in the presence of GO, GQDs and MWCNTs. According to the achieved XRD patterns, three phase of (NH4)2V12O27·xH2O, (NH4)2Co2V10O28·16 H2O and NH4V3O8 are present in the nanocomposites and well matched with JCPDS code of (00-031-0076), (00-088-0016) and (00-088-1473), respectively. The crystallite size was calculated for all samples using Scherrer equation and Williamson-Hall formula. According to the Scherrer equation, the crystallite size for prepared nanocomposites in the presence of GO, GQDs and MWCNTs are 34.35, 24.22 and 34.05 nm, respectively. To comparison the influence different carbon compounds on the lattice micro-strain and crystallite size, the Williamson-hall plot was used to determine the crystallite size for synthesized samples. Fig. 2 (a–c) shows the Williamson-Hall curve prepared nanocomposites in the presence of GO, GQDs and MWCNTs, respectively. The intercept of plotted curve of βCos (θ) versus Sin (θ) is equal to Kλ/D. The parameters of K, λ and D, are dimensionless shape factor (about 0.9), X-ray wavelength (0.154 nm for Cu) and crystallite size, correspondingly. Therefore, the derived crystallite size for prepared nanocomposites in the presence of GO, GQDs and MWCNTs are 68, 81 and 55 nm, separately. Based on obtained slope, the results show that owing to non-zero residual tension, the crystallite size determined using the Scherer equation is smaller than the crystallite size determined using Williamson-Hall plots for samples all samples.

The functional groups of prepared samples specified using FT-IR spectra which display in Fig. 3 (a–d). FT-IR spectrum for pristine sample shows in Fig. 3a. The appeared bands at 975 cm−1 related to the stretching vibrations of the terminal oxygen-vanadium bonds. The asymmetric stretching vibrations of the bridging oxygen Oxygen–vanadium bonds located at 834 and 733 cm−1. The symmetric stretching vibrations of the same bonds (VO) can be seen at 519 and 534 cm−1 [34,50]. The broad bands at 3361 cm−1 cans be assigned to asymmetric and symmetric vibrations OH which connected to the lattice water molecules. The sharp band observed at around 1621 cm−1, is due to the HOH bending vibrational modes of these water molecules. The peaks at 3170 and 1403 cm−1 are correlated to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of the NH bonds from the tetrahedral ammoniumcation, respectively [51].

According to the FT-IR spectrum of GO based nanocomposites (Fig. 3b), the broad bands at 3359 cm−1 cans be assigned to asymmetric and symmetric vibrations OH of lattice water molecules. The observed band at 2823 cm−1 linked to the symmetric stretching vibrations of the NH bonds from the tetrahedral ammoniumcation. The band located at around 1617 cm−1, is due to CC groups in the GO. The vibration of OH in GO can be seen at 1405 cm−1. The subsequent band at 1096 cm−1 related to the COC in GO. The bands at 495, 967, and 845 cm−1 are connected to the oxygen‑vanadium bonds and also at 740 cm−1 is the vibration of CH band in GO [52].

The band at 3359 cm−1 cans be assigned to asymmetric and symmetric vibrations hydroxyl function of water molecules. In addition, the detected peak at 3359 cm−1 is allocated to the vibration of OH band in GQD which is corresponded to the stretching vibration of OH. The located peak at 2457 cm−1 is related to the vibration of CH band in GQD. The aromatic ring stretching of CC and CO vibrations are located at 1619 and 1385 cm−1. The sharp bands appearing at 999 cm−1 is due to the COH. The oxygen‑vanadium bonds can be seen at 967, 825, 724 and 492 cm−1 (Fig. 3c) [53].

Fig. 3d illustrate the FT-IR spectrum for MWCNTs based nanocomposites. The displayed peak at 1618 cm−1 is owing to the HOH bending vibrational modes of these water molecules and also CO in the MWCNTs. The CO bond located at 1313 cm−1 which is related to the presence of MWCNTs in the nanocomposite texture. The observed bands at 526, 732, 826, 967 cm−1 are linked to the VO bonds. Also, the NH bridge in the vanadium-based structures are displays at 1404 and 3229 cm−1. The hydroxyl groups in the MWCNTs and vanadium-based materials as water molecules are shown at 3373 cm−1 [54].

The better specification of structure was performed using HR-TEM images which show in Fig. 6 (a, b). Based on HR-TEM images, the rod-like structure of prepared nanocomposites confirmed the facets of NH4V3O8 and (NH4)2Co2V10O28·16H2O components. The d-spacing of 0.370 nm is related to the (021) facet of NH4V3O8 component. Also, d-spacing of 0.319 nm is connected to (030) facet of (NH4)2Co2V10O28·16 H2O component.

Following the interaction between cobalt and vanadium ions, the formation of nuclei took place, leading to the subsequent aggregation of these nuclei. Through a sustained reaction over an extended period of time, tiny crystals were generated and organized gradually into rod-shaped structures, with each rod having a thickness measured in nanometers. The ammonium cobalt vanadium oxide composites, which was constructed in the presence of PEG, consists of rod-like form. The PEG serves a crucial function in the reaction process by acting as a capping agent. The presence of several active oxygen atoms in the PEG molecular chains allows metal ions to bind to PEG and create a chelating complex structure, resulting in restricted development of rods. Polyethylene glycol (PEG) may impact the surfaces of the crystal facets of a material called ammonium cobalt vanadium oxide, hence restricting undesired crystal development and promoting the formation of rods (Schematic. 1 ).

Cyclic voltammetry experiments were conducted, and the results are shown in Fig. 8 . This was conducted to explore the electrochemical characteristics of the resultant materials. MWCNT based nanocomposites exhibited higher current values for both the anodic and cathodic peaks compared to GO and GQDs based composites, which resulted in an enhancement of the electrochemical properties of the resultant composites. The values may be determined via a cyclic voltammogram or from samples collected about the electrode response, as shown in Table 2 . Consequently, the current peak for MWCNT based nanocomposites exceeds that of other samples.

Hydrogen adsorption resulting from water breakdown may be noticed as oxidation peaks in the anodic branch of the CV curves. Reduction peaks may be noticed in the cathodic branch, indicating the release of hydrogen. The current shown by the MWCNT based nanocomposites is greater than the current displayed by the GO and GQDs based nanocomposites. This suggests that the electrochemical characteristics of nanocomposites have improved due to a mutually beneficial interaction between the MWCNT components in the composite structure. However, the MWCNT based nanocomposites have the ability to move towards the center of the curve. The anodic-to-cathodic peak potential ratio serves as an indicator of the reaction's reversibility. Put simply, as the value of this ratio approaches 1, it indicates a higher likelihood of the reaction being reversible [55].

To assess the hydrogen storage capability of the electrode material composed of ammonium cobalt vanadium oxide/ammonium vanadium oxide, an electrochemical charge discharge test was conducted. The test used the chronopotentiometry method in an alkaline electrolyte, with a consistent current of 1 mA. This was conducted to investigate the hydrogen storage capacity of the materials. The discharge capacity of various nanocomposites was examined, as seen in Fig. 9 . After 15 cycles, the maximum discharge capacity of prepared nanocomposites in the presence of 10 % GO, 10 % GQDs and 10 % MWCNTs are around 28, 203 and 438 mAhg−1 respectively. Therefore, effect of MWCNT on the vanadate-based electrodes is higher than GO and GQDs and optimal electrode is MWCNT based nanocomposite. So, Effect of percentage of MWCNT was examined on the capacity of hydrogen storage which illustrate in Fig. 9(d, e). The obtained electrode materials in the presence of 5 and 20 % of MWCNT show the capacity of 1055 and 150 mAhg−1 respectively. 20 % of MWCNTs led to increasing the capacity up to 200 mAhg−1 (cycle 9) but after that capacity decreased to cycle 15 (150 mAhg−1). Fig. 10 depicts capacity change process with increasing cycle number for all samples. Thus, by increasing the amount of MWCNT, capacity decreasing was occurred. The capacity of MWCNTs based nanocomposite is enhanced by combining their physical and chemical characteristics with the structural and distinctive qualities of vanadate materials. The vanadate structures benefit from the homogeneous distribution of MWCNTs, which facilitates the efficient collection and transportation of charge carriers [56]. Table 3 compared the results of this work with different electrode materials based on metal oxides or carbon structures which reported in the previous literatures.

According to the properties of 0D, 1D, and 2D carbon structures, the performance of nanocomposites based on carbon units of various dimensions may be understood. Because of their structural characteristics and attributes, such as their tiny size and huge surface area, 0D nanomaterials have been extensively researched in the field of energy storage. In particular, their small size offers an abundance of sites for ionic adsorption. However, because 0D nanomaterials self-aggregate more easily, their electrochemical performances will be impacted. Therefore, the self-aggregation of 0D nanostructures, which closed the pores and active sites of vanadate materials, is responsible for the poorer energy storage capacity of nanocomposites in the presence of GQDs [57].

1D nanomaterials have high aspect ratios, directed growth directions, and combined effects between nano and microscales as structural characteristics. Consequently, 1D nanomaterials, which were thought to be among the most promising classes of materials for high-performance energy storage devices, are advantageous for the quick movement of electrons and ions and have an effect on tolerating stress fluctuations. As electrode materials, 1D nanomaterials may be used to increase the mobility of electrons and ions and reduce stress variation. It is commonly known that CNTs have been used in the energy storage industry because to their exceptional electrical and mechanical qualities [58].

Ionic adsorption is facilitated by the huge surface area of 2D nanomaterials, which helps to increase capacitance. Conversely, high conductivity 2D nanomaterials and adjustable interlayer spacing can improve ionic intercalation and increase electronic transmission, respectively. Because of these benefits, researchers are now favoring 2D materials. However, because of its functional groups, GO can self-aggregate or connect with vanadate material, which limits the energy storage capacity by obstructing active sites and pores [59,60].

Due to the unique one-dimensional (1D) structure of carbon nanotubes, it is possible to create macroscopic materials of different dimensions, such as 1D fibers, 2D films, and 3D foams. The carbon nanotubes possess exceptional macroscopic features characterized by their high performance. Oxides may be directly used as electrode materials or as supporting materials for capacitive electrodes in many applications. This is due to their high conductivity, wide surface area, and strong mechanical characteristics [61]. The insufficient electrical conductivity of vanadate materials leads to a quick decline, which presents a difficulty for their use in energy storage. An alternative is to use one-dimensional heterostructures as a method to enhance both electrical conductivity and structural robustness [62].

The hydrogen storage mechanism can be occurred with physisorption process and redox way. When a positive electric current is provided (with charge traveling in the positive direction), the freshly generated H+ ions adhere to the surface of the electrode material. When a negative current is applied in the opposite direction (discharge), hydrogen molecules held in the working electrodes migrate out, leading to the reformation of H2O. This procedure took place via the physisorption mechanism.

One technique to elucidate adsorption-based storage is via electrochemical mechanisms. Water acts as the proton source in the electrolyte solution at the working electrode, leading to the electron reaction that generates OH−. The hydrogen that is being produced is subsequently adsorbed onto the surface of the adsorbent (A) as described in Eq. (2), or it may recombine to create H2 molecules as described in Eqs. (3)–(5).
(1)
H 2
O +
e −
→
H ads
+
OH −
(2)
A + H →
AH ads
(3)
AH ads
+
H 2
O + e − →
H 2
+
OH −
+ A
(4)
2H →
H 2
(5)
AH ads
+
AH ads
→
H 2
+ 2A

The Eqs. (1), (3), and (4) reflect the Volmer, Heyrovsky, and Tafel reactions, respectively, at different stages of the electrochemical process [63]. Recombination of hydrogen atoms occurs, leading to the formation of molecular hydrogen by an electrochemical process. This happens when the energy released in the Tafel or Heyrovsky reactions is lower than the energy generated during the hydrogen adsorption process, and the activation barrier for any of these processes is sufficiently low. Otherwise, the hydrogen that has been adsorbed may penetrate deeper into the adsorbent and occupy positions with higher adsorption energy.

Based on redox mechanism, vanadium in obtained electrode materials has oxidation state of +5 and +4 which can reduce to valance. The reduction of the high-valence vanadium ion to a low-valence vanadium ion ensures the electrical neutrality of the oxides is maintained during ion intercalation. According to previous literatures, it is assumed that the vanadium undergoes reduction and the redox pair of vanadium plays a substantial role in hydrogen sorption. The vanadate structure achieved charge balancing by hydrogen sorption. The vanadate compound has a preference for forming hydrogen bonds in the presence of hydrogen, resulting in the creation of hydroxyl ions. This process also involves the reduction of vanadium from its +4 or +5 oxidation state to its +2 oxidation state [33].

In the current inquiry, we successfully showcased and enhanced the process of PEG-assisted hydrothermal synthesis to create rod-like vanadate materials based on (NH4)2V12O27·xH2O/(NH4)2Co2V10O28·16 H2O/NH4V3O8. The impact of the dimension of carbon nanostructures, such as carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide, and graphene quantum dots, on the electrochemical performance of hydrogen storage is investigated. These electrode materials demonstrate appealing performance and the ability to maintain high capacity. The MWCNTs based nanocomposite, demonstrated superior electrochemical properties compared to other nanocomposites. When used MWCNTs based nanocomposites with 5 % MWCNTs as the electrode material, it exhibited maximum capacity of 1055 mAhg−1 after 15 cycles at a current of 1 mA. Hence, vanadate based on (NH4)2V12O27·xH2O, (NH4)2Co2V10O28·16H2O and NH4V3O8 components with carefully controlled structure have a significant potential for the advancement of cost-effective and exceptionally efficient electrode materials for hydrogen storage in the future.

Poria Gomrokchi: Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Mehdi Shabani-Nooshabadi: Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Project administration, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Maryam Ghiyasiyan-Arani: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Data curation. Masoud Salavati-Niasari: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Professor Masoud Salavati-Niasari and co-authors of the Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, IRAN are friendly approved for providing access to the analysis facility by grant No 159271/PG1 from University of Kashan and the council of Iran National Science Foundation (INSF, 97017837). Authors would like to thank Miss Angham Husseini (SAMA Research Center) for her valuable help in making and supporting SAMA 500 Electroanalyzer instrument.
